body.text: Where to place resume keywords
Resume keywords should appear naturally across your CV.
The best places are:
1. Professional summary
Your summary can include two or three important keywords that define your fit.
Example:
Customer success specialist with experience in customer onboarding, CRM management, issue resolution, and product adoption support.
2. Skills section
Your skills section should include clear, role-specific keywords.
Example:
Customer onboarding | CRM | Account management | Retention | Client communication | Issue resolution
3. Work experience
This is the most important place to use keywords because it shows proof.
Example:
Managed customer onboarding for new accounts, tracked product adoption in CRM, and documented recurring issues for the support team.
4. Projects
If you have relevant projects, include keywords there too.
Example:
Built a Tableau dashboard to track weekly sales performance and identify pipeline trends.
5. Certifications
If the job mentions a certification and you have it, list it clearly.
Example:
Google Analytics Certification | Google
How to use keywords naturally in bullet points
The best resume bullet points combine keywords with action and evidence.
A simple formula is:
Action + keyword/task + context + result
Examples:
Weak version
Responsible for reports.
Better version
Prepared weekly sales reports using CRM data to help the team track pipeline progress.
Weak version
Worked with customers.
Better version
Supported customer onboarding by answering product questions, resolving setup issues, and documenting recurring feedback.
Weak version
Did marketing tasks.
Better version
Planned SEO-focused blog updates using keyword research and Google Analytics performance data.
Each improved version includes keywords, but the sentence still sounds natural.
That is what you want.
Examples of keyword stuffing vs natural keyword use
Example 1: Marketing role
Bad:
SEO expert with SEO experience in SEO strategy, SEO content, SEO analytics, SEO performance, SEO optimization, and SEO campaigns.
Better:
Planned SEO content updates using keyword research, search intent analysis, and Google Analytics data to improve organic traffic.
Example 2: Project coordinator role
Bad:
Project management, project planning, project coordination, project tracking, project communication, project delivery.
Better:
Coordinated project timelines, tracked task progress in Jira, and shared weekly updates with cross-functional stakeholders.
Example 3: Customer success role
Bad:
Customer success, customer onboarding, customer support, customer retention, customer communication, customer issues.
Better:
Supported customer onboarding, resolved account issues, and used CRM notes to track adoption risks and follow-up actions.
The better versions are stronger because they show experience, not just vocabulary.
How many keywords should your resume include?
There is no perfect number.
A good rule is to include the most important keywords naturally, not repeatedly.
For most job applications, choose:

items: 5-10 core keywords from the job description

body.text: For example, a sales operations job might focus on:
You do not need to repeat these in every section.
Use them where they make sense.
Should you copy exact phrases from the job description?
Sometimes, yes, but carefully.
If the job description uses a standard phrase that accurately describes your experience, you can use it.
For example:
These are normal professional terms.
But do not copy full sentences or entire requirement lists from the job description.
That can make your CV feel lazy or dishonest.
Instead, adapt the language to your real experience.
Job description phrase:
Manage customer onboarding and improve product adoption across new accounts.
Resume bullet:
Supported customer onboarding for new accounts by answering setup questions, tracking adoption risks, and sharing feedback with the product team.
This is natural, specific, and honest.
Do soft skills count as resume keywords?
Yes, but they are usually weaker unless supported by examples.
Soft skills include:
These words are common, so listing them alone may not help much.
Instead of writing:
Strong communication skills.
Show communication through experience:
Prepared weekly status updates for stakeholders and coordinated follow-up actions across sales, support, and operations teams.
This proves the skill instead of just naming it.
How to avoid keyword stuffing
Use these rules:
Rule 1: Do not repeat the same keyword too often
Use natural variation when appropriate.
For example:
Rule 2: Put keywords inside real achievements
Keywords should appear in meaningful bullet points.
Rule 3: Avoid long keyword lists
A skills section is useful, but it should not become a wall of words.
Rule 4: Keep your CV readable for humans
Recruiters should be able to understand your CV quickly.
Rule 5: Be honest
Never add a keyword just because it appears in the job description.
If you cannot explain it in an interview, do not include it.
Resume keyword checklist
Before submitting your CV, check:

items: Did you read the job description carefully?
Did you identify required skills and repeated terms?
Are the keywords connected to your real experience?
Did you include important tools and platforms?
Did you use keywords in your summary, skills, and experience?
Do your bullet points prove your skills?
Did you avoid repeating the same keyword unnaturally?
Does the resume still sound human?
Is the format simple and ATS-friendly?

A small mistake can make a strong candidate look careless. A missing keyword can make your CV seem less relevant. A confusing layout can make it harder for applicant tracking systems and recruiters to understand your experience.
The good news is that you do not need to rewrite your entire resume before every application.
You need a simple checklist.

Use this resume checklist before you submit your next job application. It will help you catch common problems, improve readability, strengthen your bullet points, and make your CV more relevant to the role.
The fastest place to start is to tailor your resume before applying so the document reflects the role in front of you.

body.heading: Check and tailor your CV before you apply.
body.text: Upload your resume, paste the job description, and get a polished, ATS-friendly version in minutes.
Why you should check your resume before every application
Many job seekers send the same resume to every job.
That feels faster, but it can reduce your chances.

Each job description has different priorities. One role may care about leadership. Another may care about reporting. Another may care about customer communication, technical tools, project ownership, or industry experience.
A quick resume review helps you make sure your CV is:
You do not need to change everything.
But you should check the details that affect whether your resume gets noticed.
The 25-point resume checklist
Use this checklist before you apply.
1. Is your resume tailored to the job description?
Your resume should not feel like a generic document.
Read the job description and ask:

items: Does my summary match this role?
Are the most important skills visible?
Do my bullet points reflect the job's responsibilities?
Have I included relevant keywords naturally?

body.text: If the answer is no, customize your CV before applying.
2. Is your professional summary specific?
Avoid summaries that could apply to anyone.
Weak example:
Motivated professional with strong communication and teamwork skills.
Better example:
Customer success specialist with experience supporting onboarding, resolving account issues, maintaining CRM records, and improving product adoption.
Your summary should quickly explain who you are and why you fit this specific role.
3. Are the right resume keywords included?
Resume keywords help connect your CV to the job description.
Look for keywords related to:
Use them naturally in your summary, skills section, and work experience.
4. Is your resume ATS-friendly?
If you are applying online, your resume may be processed by an applicant tracking system.
Check that your CV uses:
Avoid relying on graphics, icons, images, or complex layouts to communicate important information. An ATS-friendly resume keeps your content readable.
5. Is the format simple and readable?
A clean format helps recruiters scan your CV quickly.
Use:
Avoid visual clutter. A simple resume format is easier for both recruiters and software to read.
6. Is your contact information correct?
Check your:
Make sure all links work.
Use a professional email address.
7. Is your file name professional?
Avoid file names like:
Use a clear file name:
A clear file name looks more professional and helps avoid confusion.
8. Did you follow the employer's file instructions?
Some employers ask for PDF. Others ask for DOCX.
Always follow the application instructions.
If no format is specified, use a clean PDF or DOCX version. The layout matters more than the file type.
9. Are your job titles, companies, and dates clear?
Every work experience entry should clearly show:
Example:
Marketing Specialist, Brightline Media | Istanbul, Turkey | March 2022 - Present
Do not make recruiters guess your timeline.
10. Are your bullet points achievement-focused?
Many resumes list tasks instead of showing value.
Weak example:
Responsible for reports.
Better example:
Prepared weekly performance reports, summarized key trends, and shared insights with the sales team.
Strong bullet points show action, context, and impact.
11. Did you add numbers where possible?
Numbers make achievements more concrete.
Examples:
Do not invent metrics.
If you do not have exact numbers, use truthful context:
Supported onboarding for multiple enterprise clients.
12. Are your most relevant achievements near the top?
Recruiters scan quickly.
Put your strongest and most relevant bullet points first under each role.
If the job focuses on project coordination, do not hide your project coordination experience under unrelated tasks.
Lead with relevance.
13. Did you remove outdated or irrelevant details?
Your resume does not need to include everything.
Remove or shorten:

items: Basic tasks that do not add value

body.text: A focused resume is usually stronger than a crowded one.
14. Is your skills section focused?
Your skills section should match the job.
Avoid listing too many unrelated skills.
For example, if you are applying for a data analyst role, prioritize:
SQL, Excel, Tableau, data cleaning, reporting, dashboarding, Python
If you are applying for a customer success role, prioritize:
Customer onboarding, CRM, account management, retention, issue resolution, client communication
The right skills should be easy to find.
15. Are soft skills supported by examples?
Soft skills matter, but listing them alone is not enough.
Instead of only writing:
Communication, leadership, teamwork
Show them in your bullet points:
Coordinated weekly updates between sales, product, and support teams to keep customer issues visible and improve follow-up speed.
Proof is stronger than a list.
16. Are your verbs strong and active?
Start bullet points with action verbs.
Examples:
Avoid weak phrases like:
You can still use "supported" when it is accurate, especially for junior or collaborative roles. Just make the rest of the bullet specific.
17. Is your resume easy to scan in 10 seconds?
A recruiter should be able to quickly understand:
If your resume feels dense or unfocused, simplify it.
Use shorter paragraphs, bullet points, and clearer headings.
18. Is your resume length appropriate?
For most candidates, one to two pages is enough.
One page may work well for:

items: Career changers with limited related experience

body.text: Two pages may work well for:

items: Candidates with many relevant projects or achievements

body.text: Do not cut useful content just to force one page.
But do not keep irrelevant content just to look experienced.
19. Is your education section in the right place?
If you are a student or recent graduate, education may appear near the top.
If you have several years of work experience, education usually goes below experience.
Include:
Do not overfill the education section if your work experience is stronger.
20. Are certifications and projects relevant?
Certifications and projects can strengthen your CV if they match the job.
Include certifications that are:
Include projects if they show practical skills, especially for students, career changers, technical candidates, or portfolio-based roles.
21. Did you check grammar and spelling?
Typos can make your resume look rushed.
Check:
Read your resume out loud or use a grammar tool before sending.
22. Are dates and tenses consistent?
Use past tense for previous roles:
Managed weekly reporting.
Use present tense for current responsibilities:
Manage weekly reporting.
Keep date formatting consistent:
Choose one style and use it throughout.
23. Did you avoid unnecessary personal information?
In most cases, your resume does not need:

items: Personal photo, unless expected in your market

body.text: Keep the focus on professional qualifications.
24. Does your resume match your LinkedIn profile?
Your resume and LinkedIn do not need to be identical, but they should not contradict each other.
Check that your:
Recruiters may check both.
25. Would you feel confident discussing every line?
Before sending your resume, ask:
Can I explain this clearly in an interview?
If you added a skill, tool, metric, or achievement, be ready to talk about it.
Do not include anything you cannot honestly support.
A strong resume is not only optimized.
It is truthful and interview-ready.
Quick resume checklist summary
Before applying, make sure your resume is:

items: Saved with a clear file name

body.text: This final check can take just a few minutes, but it can make your application much stronger.

A resume checklist helps you apply with more confidence.
You do not need to rebuild your CV every time. You just need to make sure it is relevant, readable, accurate, and aligned with the job description.

articles.title: ATS Resume Template: A Simple Structure You Can Use Today
articles.description: Use this simple ATS resume template to create a clean, readable CV with a clear summary, skills, experience, education, certifications, and projects.

body.text: A resume does not need to be complicated to be effective.
In fact, when you apply online, simple is often better.

Many companies use applicant tracking systems, also called ATS, to collect and organize resumes. These systems may scan your CV for contact details, work experience, skills, education, dates, certifications, and keywords from the job description.
If your resume uses a complicated design, important information may be harder to read.
That is why an ATS resume template should be clean, structured, and easy to scan.
In this guide, you will get a simple ATS-friendly resume template you can use today, plus tips for writing each section clearly.

body.heading: Create an ATS-friendly CV from your existing resume.
body.text: Upload your CV, paste the job description, and get a tailored version in minutes.
What is an ATS resume template?
An ATS resume template is a resume structure designed to be easy for applicant tracking systems and recruiters to read.
It usually avoids:
Instead, it uses:
The goal is not to make your resume boring.
The goal is to make your experience clear.
A clean template helps your skills, achievements, and qualifications stand out without being hidden behind design. It should follow an ATS-friendly resume format.
Simple ATS resume template
Use this structure as your base:

items: Email | Phone | City, Country | LinkedIn | Portfolio or Website
PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY: A short 2-4 line summary focused on your target role, relevant experience, key skills, and strongest value.
SKILLS: Skill 1 | Skill 2 | Skill 3 | Skill 4 | Skill 5 | Tool 1 | Tool 2 | Methodology
Job Title, Company Name | City, Country | Month Year - Month Year
Bullet point showing a relevant responsibility, action, and result.
Bullet point using keywords from the job description naturally.
Bullet point showing a measurable achievement or clear business impact.
Bullet point showing collaboration, tools, or role-specific experience.
EDUCATION: Degree or Program, School Name | City, Country | Graduation Year
PROJECTS: Project Name | Tool, Skill, or Context
Short bullet point explaining what you built, improved, analyzed, managed, or delivered.

body.text: This template works because it is simple, readable, and easy to customize.
Why this ATS resume template works
This structure works well because it puts the most important information where recruiters expect to find it.
Your contact information appears at the top.
Your summary explains your fit.
Your skills section makes key qualifications easy to scan.
Your work experience proves your value.
Your education, certifications, and projects support your application.
It also uses standard headings, which can help both recruiters and hiring systems understand the document.
A good template should not force the reader to figure out where everything is.
It should make your experience easy to follow.
Section 1: Contact information
Your contact information should be simple and clear.
Use this format:
FIRSTNAME LASTNAME, email@example.com | +90 555 555 55 55 | Istanbul, Turkey | linkedin.com/in/name | portfolio.com
Include:

items: Portfolio, GitHub, or website, if relevant

body.text: Avoid:
Your contact information should be written as normal text so it is easy to copy, read, and parse.
Section 2: Professional summary
Your professional summary should quickly answer:
Why are you a good fit for this role?
Keep it short. Two to four lines are enough.
A weak summary sounds generic:
Motivated and hardworking professional with strong communication skills and a passion for success.
A stronger summary is specific:

Customer success specialist with experience supporting customer onboarding, resolving account issues, maintaining CRM records, and improving product adoption through clear communication and follow-up.
The second summary is better because it includes role-specific information.
It tells the recruiter what kind of work you have done and why it matters.
This is also where you can tailor your resume to the job description quickly.

links.text: tailor your resume to the job description

body.text: Section 3: Skills
Your skills section should be focused on the job you are applying for.
Do not list every skill you have.
List the most relevant skills, tools, and methods from the job description that match your real experience.
Example for a marketing role:
SEO | Content strategy | Google Analytics | Keyword research | Campaign reporting | Email marketing | Conversion optimization
Example for a project coordinator role:
Project coordination | Stakeholder communication | Jira | Task tracking | Reporting | Documentation | Agile workflows
Example for a customer success role:
Customer onboarding | CRM | Account management | Issue resolution | Product adoption | Client communication | Retention support
Your skills section should be easy to scan.
It should also connect to your work experience. If you list a skill, try to show evidence of that skill in your bullet points. This is where resume keywords matter.
Section 4: Work experience
Your work experience section is usually the most important part of your resume.
Use this structure:

items: Company Name | City, Country | Month Year - Month Year
Action verb + keyword or tool + responsibility + outcome.

body.text: For example:
Marketing Specialist, Brightline Media | Istanbul, Turkey | March 2022 - April 2025

items: Planned SEO-focused blog content using keyword research, search intent analysis, and performance data.
Managed campaign calendars across blog, email, and social media channels to improve launch consistency.
Prepared monthly marketing reports summarizing traffic, engagement, and conversion trends.
Collaborated with sales and product teams to align campaign messaging with customer needs.

body.text: This format is strong because each bullet point is specific.
It shows what the candidate did, how they did it, and why it mattered.
How to write ATS-friendly bullet points
A strong resume bullet point should be clear, specific, and relevant.
Use this formula:
Action + task + tool/context + result
Examples:
Weak: Responsible for reports.
Better: Prepared weekly performance reports using CRM data to help the sales team track pipeline progress.
Weak: Helped customers.
Better: Supported customer onboarding by answering setup questions, resolving account issues, and documenting recurring feedback.
Weak: Worked on marketing.
Better: Planned SEO content updates using keyword research and Google Analytics data to improve organic visibility.
Good bullet points help both ATS systems and recruiters because they include relevant terms in a natural way.
Section 5: Education
Your education section can be simple.
Use this format:
EDUCATION: Bachelor of Science in Business Administration, Marmara University | Istanbul, Turkey | 2023
Include:

items: Relevant coursework, if you are early career

body.text: If you are a student or recent graduate, education can appear above work experience.
If you have several years of relevant experience, education usually belongs below work experience.
Section 6: Certifications
Certifications can help if they are relevant to the role.
Use this format:
Include certifications that are:

items: Connected to a skill you want to highlight

body.text: Avoid listing outdated or unrelated certificates that distract from the role.
Section 7: Projects
Projects are useful if they show relevant skills.
They are especially helpful for:
Use this format:
PROJECTS: Sales Dashboard Project | Excel, Tableau, Data Analysis
Built a dashboard to track weekly sales performance, identify pipeline trends, and summarize insights for decision-making.
A good project entry should explain what you made, which tools you used, what problem it solved, and what result or learning came from it.
Keep it short and relevant.
Should your ATS resume be one page or two pages?
There is no perfect length for everyone.
A one-page resume may be best if you are:

items: Changing careers and focusing only on transferable experience

body.text: A two-page resume may be better if you have:
The goal is not to make your resume short.
The goal is to make it focused.
Do not cut useful content just to reach one page. But do not keep weak or irrelevant content just to fill space.
ATS resume template example
Here is a complete example using the template:

items: Marketing specialist with experience planning SEO content, managing campaign calendars, analyzing performance data, and improving organic visibility across digital channels.
SEO | Content strategy | Google Analytics | Campaign reporting | Email marketing | Keyword research | Performance tracking
Marketing Specialist, Brightline Media | Istanbul, Turkey | March 2022 - April 2025
Planned SEO-focused blog content using keyword research, search intent analysis, and performance data.
Managed campaign calendars across blog, email, and social media channels to improve launch consistency.
Prepared monthly marketing reports summarizing traffic, engagement, and conversion trends.
Collaborated with sales and product teams to align campaign messaging with customer needs.
Marketing Intern, Northline Studio | Istanbul, Turkey | June 2021 - February 2022
Supported social media scheduling, campaign reporting, and competitor research for weekly marketing updates.
Organized campaign assets and helped maintain the content calendar across multiple channels.
BA in Communication, Istanbul University | Istanbul, Turkey | 2021

body.text: This example is simple, focused, and readable.
It includes relevant keywords, but it does not stuff them unnaturally.

ATS resume template for students or recent graduates
If you have limited work experience, use this structure:

items: Email | Phone | City, Country | LinkedIn | Portfolio
PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY: Short summary focused on your target role, relevant coursework, projects, internships, and skills.
SKILLS: Skill 1 | Skill 2 | Tool 1 | Tool 2 | Methodology
EDUCATION: Degree or Program, School Name | City, Country | Graduation Year
PROJECTS: Project Name | Tools or Skills
EXPERIENCE: Job Title or Internship, Company Name | Dates

body.text: For early-career candidates, projects and education may be more important than work history.
The key is to show practical ability, not just coursework.
ATS resume template for career changers
If you are changing careers, use this structure:

items: Email | Phone | City, Country | LinkedIn | Portfolio
PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY: Short summary connecting your previous experience to your target role.
SKILLS: Transferable Skill 1 | Role-specific Skill 1 | Tool 1 | Communication | Analysis | Project coordination
RELEVANT EXPERIENCE: Job Title, Company Name | Dates
PROJECTS OR TRAINING: Project, Course, or Certification
ADDITIONAL EXPERIENCE: Older or less relevant role
EDUCATION: Degree or Program, School Name | Year

body.text: For career changers, the summary and skills sections are especially important.
They help explain the connection between where you have been and where you want to go. A job-specific resume makes that connection clearer.
Common ATS template mistakes to avoid
Avoid these mistakes when using an ATS resume template.
Mistake 1: Choosing design over clarity
A beautiful template is not useful if it hides your experience.
Mistake 2: Using two columns for important content
Two-column layouts can look modern, but they may create reading-order issues.
Mistake 3: Adding skill bars
Skill bars are vague and not very useful.
Instead of showing "Excel: 90%," list Excel as a skill and prove it in your experience.
Mistake 4: Using icons instead of words
Icons can be misunderstood or ignored.
Use text for important information.
Mistake 5: Keeping the same template content for every job
The template can stay the same, but the content should change based on the role. Use a resume checklist before applying.
How to customize this template for each job
The structure can stay consistent, but the content should be tailored.
Before each application:

items: Rewrite your most relevant bullet points.
Check the format and file name.

body.text: This usually takes much less time than rewriting your resume from scratch.
The template gives you the structure.
The job description tells you what to emphasize.

You need to choose a clean format, find the right keywords, update your summary, improve your bullet points, and make sure the final version matches the job description.
JobSpecificCV helps simplify that process.

body.text: Create an ATS-friendly CV from your existing resume. Upload your CV, paste the job description, and get a tailored version in minutes.
Final thoughts
An ATS resume template should make your experience easier to understand.
It does not need heavy design, graphics, or complicated formatting.
Use a simple structure: contact information, professional summary, skills, work experience, education, certifications, and projects.
Keep your headings clear. Use relevant keywords naturally. Write bullet points that show real experience. Avoid design elements that make your CV harder to parse.
The best resume template is not the most decorative one.
It is the one that helps your qualifications get noticed.
